author: @michaelvanallen6400
text:
*All practical tests of ground-based small wind turbines show the same thing: 1) They hardly produce any electricity. 2) They do not last long.*
Wind/electricity yield increases with the third power of the wind speed.+ 100% wind = +800% electricity. Close to the ground, however, there is 0 constant wind, but permanent gusts. *Solar panels generate at least 10 times as much electricity for the same investment of time and money.* Maintenance-free for at least 25 years!
On the other hand, 9 out of 10 small wind turbines stop working after a while due to the extremely gusty winds.

author: @michaelvanallen6400
text:
*All practical tests of ground-based small wind turbines show: 1) They hardly produce any electricity! 2) They do not last long!* 
Electricity yield is proportional to third power of wind speed: +100% wind generates +800% more electricity! Close to ground: 0 constant wind!
*On the other hand, solar panels generate at least 10 times more electricity with the same investment in time & money!*  Reliable and almost maintenance-free for at least 25 years!  9 out of 10 small wind turbines stop working after just a few years due to the extremely gusty winds close to ground.

author: @tom4ivo
text: Back in the 70s and 80s, home renewable energy enthusiasts ranked hydro as their top choice, wind as second, and solar last.  Why?  If you have flowing water and and a drop of 20 feet or more, you can generate a lot of power from a small turbine, because water is heavy.  Wind doesn't have as much energy as falling water, but a few hours of the wind blowing can generate as much electricity as a bunch of solar panels generating all day.
I enjoy this channel more then your others do to the lack of 3d printing and I thank you for it

author: @stevetaylor2818
text: I like your videos, but your math on energy provided by the Sun is out by a large magnitude: Each hour 430 quintillion Joules of energy from the sun hits the Earth. In comparison, the total amount of energy that all humans use in a year is 410 quintillion Joules. The Sun provides around 10,000 times the energy we consume (of course we could never capture it all, but only need a very tiny percentage)

author: @JustAnotherBigby
text:
I really enjoy your videos.

This is a side point to your main content here, but I think your bit about 20 TW from the sun in incorrect.   Looking at https://www.energy.gov/articles/top-6-things-you-didnt-know-about-solar-energy#:~:text=Solar%20energy%20is%20the%20most,the%20world's%20total%20energy%20use, the numbers is more like 178,000 TW.   The IEA has global consumption at ~23TW-hours,  that's far less than 178k TW-years.  Did I misunderstand?

author: @Etheoma
text:
Dude... Ya missread the graphic, it's 20TW PER HOUR coming in vs useage of 16TWh PER YEAR usage so more energy hits the earth in an hour than we use in a year admiteddly solar PV pannels are only ~21% efficent, so you "only" need to cover 876th of the Earth in solar pannels to create 20TWh per year kinda, it's going to be a little bit more because I took (24*365.25)= 8765.76 which 20% of which is 1753.152 then devided by 2 because it's going to be night half the time.

It would probably end up being closer to 16TW as during the morning and the evening a lot of the sunslight is going to be mostly blocked by the atmosphere, yeh I know it's scattered, but it has the same effect and saying you only need to cover 0.114% of the Earth in solar pannels is no small deal, as 71% of the Earth is water and you are going to want to put them on land so that already has you bumped upto 0.4% of land on the surface, and you are going to want it near population centres which are almost always near land which is famable so you are going to end up having to put solar pannels on land which is sutiable for farming which is only 38% of land so you are going to end up using about 0.5% of farmland.

which this all doesn't sound that unmanagable until you realize that 0.4% of land is 229,075.6 square miles, yeh sure distrobuting it across the planet like you would need to do anyway makes that number sound less rediculious as well as using roof tops etc, but just producing that much square milage of solar pannels is a stupidly large undertaking, then you also have to link the planet with high voltage DV lines which is another stupidly large project and you are going to need to increare our energy storage by orders of magnitude.

So I'm not saying you don't need to diversify how we are producing energy, I'm saying that it is technically possible to power our planet off solar pannels without covering a signficant percentage of our planet in solar pannels would it be optimal or even practical, no... but it is techically doable.

Also I would like to point out that evacuated solar thermal tubes are 100% efficent at collecting sunlight to, so in colder places in the world that do get sunlight all year around and most of their energy useage goes on heating you can significantly reduce that area of solar pannels needed also improving the efficency of heat pumps by raising the refirgerant tempeture before it goes through the compressor, this is only worthwhile if you regularly get tempetures below 5C.

author: @hunnybunnysheavymetalmusic6542
text:
Use steel cans, or plastic barrels and you can keep the cost very low.

While metal cans are heavier and harder to work with [potentially razor sharp edges, a lot of weight, and the need for stronger tools] they can potentially withstand more wind force.

On the other hand, plastic barrels are lighter, won't rust, tends not to make razor sharp edges, and to bend vanes, one can use a heat gun and some pieces of wood to safely soften and bend the vanes, making them a lot safer and easier to build and install.

replies.author: @PoweredbyRobots
replies.text: @PseudoSarcasm so the priority is isolating the turbine from the house via a suspension type system. Using an air type shock absorber would give extra sound deadening benefits. It'll be a lot quieter than air con by that point, in the house at any rate.

author: @AORD72
text:
The is a reason we don't have wind turbines on our houses, because it is not cost effective. We have had winds turbines for hundreds of years, residential areas don't have enough wind energy to make small wind turbines economical. Do the maths, calculate the energy in the wind for a square meter and see for your self yourself. For most cities it is like 15 Watts a square meter,  and a turbine will only be about 30% efficient, so like 5 Watts. 
Read the year long studies (with,actual data) on wind turbines in residential areas and see small turbines will never work.

author: @cliffmullen6751
text: Interestingly, for me anyway, is that this video just eliminated the need for a bunch of pre-experimental measurements. Watching the wind whipping snow over the ridge of my barn during a storm last winter, it occurred to me the wind was behaving like it was passing through a venturi (which, logically, it is doing when you consider air pressure as an opposing force to the piitch of the roof). I think, after watching this, my private theory is pretty well affirmed, and i can move on to tinkering with how i might go about capturing that wind energy. Thanks for this one

author: @donniewatson9120
text: Where I grew up, West Texas on the south plains, there is always a wind. There is rarely anything less than 5 meters a second. The wind averages around 7 meters a second with gusts over 15 meters a second on a regular day. Sandstorms out there often have 30 to 35 meters a second. This is at ground level. Get up 25 feet and the average speed is higher.

author: @Ken00001010
text: Yes, you can easily measure the torque because it is proportional to the generated current while the RPM is proportional to the voltage. You can easily make a dial switch with a range of load resistors and measure the voltage stepping through the range. Plot that (V^2/R) on paper and you will find a max power point giving you the info you want. Or, just hook it up to a MPPT (maximum power point tracking) controller used for solar, and it will do that same thing for you automatically.

author: @affiliatereviews4079
text: Using those hyperbolic funnels you made for the tesla turbine would work really well with this Ridgeline idea. You could use them like you did for the ship video, staticly, and funnel the wind into your rose turbine you made , or just like the funnels turn. It should work really well.

replies.author: @AORD72
replies.text: They don't work. Wind follows the path of least resistance, the wind just follows around the funnel. We have had hundreds oof years of wind turbines and there are none with funnels because they block the wind, they don't work.

replies.author: @SynomDroni
replies.text: The rear of the roof creates sub-pressure. A shroud could work. Not only getting the wind in, but taking the air away is crucial. The advantage of a shroud I see in aerodynamics. A bid funnel at the front will create more resistance and incrrases risk of wind damage, and/or demands stronger build, i.e. cost. I see my house with a slick spoiler, like a Bat Mobile.

replies.author: @AORD72
replies.text: @SynomDroni "shroud could work", no, a shroud just creates wind resistance. The wind follows the path of least pressure, that is around the outside of the shroud.

replies.author: @SynomDroni
replies.text: @AORD72 sorry, I meant more like a spoiler. Basically sucking the air through

replies.author: @AORD72
replies.text: @SynomDroni Same problem, you are trying to increase pressure in one location (at the turbine), this doesn't work. The reason is the wind takes the path of least pressure, which won't be where you are trying to increase the pressure.

author: @colleenforrest7936
text:
I love those roof top wind generators! Its definately in my future hone design.  I printed a ugrinski that Ive been playing with.  I think theres a lot we can learn about wing scavaging by looking at how wind travels over surfaces and maximizing those effects instead of making things more aerodynamic.  

The more I look at solar, i think we are using the wrong type. We should be using solar thermal instead and storing it in a thermal mass like a sand battery or concrete instead of a standard battery.  I was looking ar a new house build the other day and particularly at the concrete slab under the garage. Say you put another 3 feet of concrete under that and thermally insulate it from the garage floor above. That would be a perfect place to store and syphon your heat from without taking up more space in the yard.  Something you'd obviously want to do with a new build.  I'm not tearing my garage down!

replies.author: @Struthio_Camelus
replies.text:
The Norwegians have made progress using sand for a heat battery - it may be that a large concrete block is neither needed nor desirable from an economic or environmental standpoint.

Sand is far cheaper, and embodies no carbon footprint.

replies.author: @colleenforrest7936
replies.text:
@Struthio_Camelus it might not be concrete but something that makes a good thermal storage and will probably be a variety of materials if we want enough for a massive solution.  There's a sand shortage happening and our beaches and seas need sand.  Even though concrete uses sand it uses other things so would be less of an impact on the sand economy.  Considering someone's already done the math to figure out that there's not enough lithium on the planet for everyone to have a power wall, we need to do the math to understand the feasibility and environmental impacts of distributing a sufficient thermal mass solution at scale.
I don't think concrete is going to be the final answer either.  Maybe just thermally isolating a portion of the natural clay or bedrock that's already on property would work???

replies.author: @Struthio_Camelus
replies.text: @colleenforrest7936 There are different grades and sources of sand. Beach sand and desert are distinct from each other and are also different from mason sand (which is in short supply and used as a major component of concrete.)

replies.author: @sailaway8244
replies.text: You don't need to tear your garage down , consider a subsurface reservoir below decking , surface level a reservoir in a retaining wall or raised feature or above ground a wardrobe type structure on a party line (probably made of insulated S/S or Aluminum ) ...,wind generation direct too stored resistive load would give 98% efficiency (minus insulation losses) and safe storage of 300 C° without risk of burning your garage down

replies.author: @colleenforrest7936
replies.text: @Struthio_Camelus okay, so it's just that type of sand that's at issue. A heat storage application wouldn't need those special properties.  I was also thinking about demolished buildings. If the old brick and concrete could be crushed and used like the sand.
